title: Occupant Safety During Water Damage Restoration — Relocation, Air Quality & Access Restrictions in Australia
description: A practical guide to managing occupant safety during water damage restoration in Australia — when to recommend temporary relocation, air quality monitoring requirements, access restrictions for Category 2 and 3 jobs, and documenting safety decisions.
author: Phill McGurk | RestoreAssist
transcript:
## Occupant Safety Is a Professional Obligation

The restoration technician has both a legal and professional duty of care to occupants of the building being restored. Under Australian WHS legislation, the restorer as a PCBU (Person Conducting a Business or Undertaking) must manage risks to the health and safety of all persons at the workplace — including building occupants who are not workers.

IICRC S500:2025 and S520:2023 both address occupant safety, emphasising that the restorer must assess the suitability of continued occupancy before commencing work and must communicate clearly with the insured about any safety concerns.

## When Temporary Relocation Is Recommended

Temporary displacement of occupants is typically necessary when:

**Category 3 contamination**: Sewage or flood water contamination presents direct biological hazards to occupants, particularly vulnerable individuals (children under 5, elderly, immunocompromised). IICRC S500:2025 strongly recommends temporary relocation during active Category 3 remediation. Most Australian insurers include a temporary accommodation allowance under the policy for displacement caused by unliveable conditions.

**Mould remediation**: Active mould remediation with containment and negative air creates elevated airborne spore loads in adjacent areas. Occupants with asthma, allergic rhinitis, or compromised immune function are particularly at risk. Temporary displacement is recommended for the duration of active mould remediation.

**Structural compromise**: Any condition where structural elements (ceilings, floors, walls) may fail creates an inherent safety risk to occupants. Ceiling cavities saturated with water are at risk of sudden collapse.

**Air quality impacts**: Drying and remediation equipment generates noise, dust, and altered air conditions. Continuous operation of equipment in an occupied dwelling is disruptive and may have air quality implications from VOC off-gassing of cleaning agents.

## Communicating Safety Decisions

The restorer should communicate safety recommendations to the insured in writing. A verbal recommendation to vacate is not adequate protection if the occupant is later harmed and claims they were not informed. The recommendation should:

- State clearly what the identified safety concern is
- Explain why temporary displacement is recommended
- Reference the applicable standard or regulation supporting the recommendation
- Note that the final decision rests with the insured (you cannot force relocation)
- Be signed and dated by the restorer and, ideally, acknowledged by the insured

If the insured declines relocation despite a documented safety recommendation, note this refusal in writing and report it to the insurer.

## Air Quality Management During Restoration

For occupied dwellings where displacement is not occurring, air quality management includes:

- Using HEPA-filtered air scrubbers to reduce airborne particulate loads in the affected area
- Isolating the work area from occupied areas of the dwelling with poly sheeting and negative pressure
- Scheduling heavy-dust activities (demolition, abrasive cleaning) during periods when occupants are not home
- Ventilating cleaning agent fumes before occupants re-enter treated areas
- Avoiding the use of highly volatile cleaning agents (high-VOC products) in occupied spaces

## Access Restrictions

The work area must be clearly demarcated with physical barriers and signage restricting access to authorised personnel. Children must be prevented from accessing work areas containing sharp debris, chemical agents, or biological contamination. Any access restriction on a Category 3 job must be maintained until post-remediation clearance is confirmed.
videoScript:
G'day, welcome to RestoreAssist. Today we're talking about occupant safety during restoration — a topic that has real legal and ethical weight, and that's worth taking seriously on every job.

As a restoration company — a PCBU under Australian WHS law — you have a duty of care to everyone at the worksite, including building occupants who are not your workers. That duty extends to communicating safety concerns clearly and recommending displacement when conditions warrant it.

So when should you be recommending temporary relocation?

Category 3 contamination is the clearest case. Sewage or flood water contamination presents real biological hazards. Vulnerable occupants — children under 5, elderly, anyone immunocompromised — are at meaningful risk from continued exposure during active remediation. Most Australian insurers include a temporary accommodation allowance for displacement caused by unliveable conditions. Help the insured make the claim for it.

Active mould remediation with containment and negative air creates elevated airborne spore concentrations in adjacent areas. Anyone with asthma, allergic rhinitis, or compromised immunity should be out of the building for the duration of active work.

Saturated ceilings are a structural risk — they can and do collapse under the weight of water. If you've got a ceiling void that's taken on significant water, the occupants need to be out of rooms below it until the risk is assessed.

When you make a safety recommendation for displacement, make it in writing. A verbal 'you might want to consider leaving' does not protect you if someone is subsequently harmed. Document the concern, the reason for the recommendation, and the applicable standard behind it. If the insured declines to vacate despite your written recommendation, note their refusal in writing and report it to the insurer.

For occupied dwellings where displacement isn't happening, manage air quality by running HEPA-filtered air scrubbers, physically isolating the work area with poly and negative pressure, scheduling dusty work when occupants are away, and avoiding high-VOC cleaning agents in occupied spaces.

Access restrictions on the work area are non-negotiable for Category 3 — physical barriers, signage, no children in the work zone. Keep them in place until clearance is confirmed.

RestoreAssist includes an occupant safety checklist in the initial inspection workflow so these decisions are documented as part of the standard inspection record.

Head to restoreassist.app.
